blogPosts.title: Your Website Should Run Itself: The Case for Autonomous CMS
blogPosts.excerpt: Content management has not evolved in 20 years. It is time for a system that operates autonomously — writing, publishing, qualifying leads, and learning from every interaction.

content.text: For two decades, content management systems have asked humans to do everything: write content, publish pages, respond to leads, send newsletters, manage bookings. The CMS was a tool. You were the operator.
The Problem With Tools
Tools require operators. Every blog post needs someone to write it. Every lead needs someone to qualify it. Every campaign needs someone to schedule it. The workload scales linearly with ambition.
What If the CMS Was the Operator?
FlowPilot is not a feature inside a CMS. It is the CMS. An autonomous AI agent that:
Writes and publishes content based on your objectives
Qualifies leads the moment they arrive — day or night
Sends campaigns triggered by real visitor behavior
Books meetings and follows up automatically
Reflects on its own performance on a regular schedule and improves
The Autonomous Loop

FlowPilot operates in a continuous cycle: Heartbeat → Reflect → Plan → Execute → Log → Learn. On a configurable schedule, it reviews what happened, what worked, what failed, and adjusts its approach. No human intervention required.
You Set Objectives. FlowPilot Executes.

Instead of managing tasks, you set goals: "Publish 4 blog posts per month." "Qualify all inbound leads within 5 minutes." "Send a weekly newsletter." FlowPilot breaks these into actions and executes them autonomously.
Human-in-the-Loop, Not Human-in-the-Way
Every action FlowPilot takes is logged. Sensitive actions require your approval. You review an activity feed, not a task list. You direct strategy, not operations.

meta.description: Discover why autonomous CMS is the future. Learn how FlowPilot operates your entire online presence — content, leads, campaigns — while you focus on strategy.

blogPosts.title: FlowPilot: An Autonomous Agent for Your Presence
blogPosts.excerpt: Meet FlowPilot — an OpenClaw-inspired autonomous agent with persistent memory, self-evolving skills, and goal-driven objectives that manages content, CRM, email, and e-commerce.

content.text: FlowPilot is not a chatbot. It is not an AI assistant. It is an autonomous digital operator inspired by the OpenClaw framework — designed to run your website, qualify your leads, publish your content, and learn from every interaction.
The Six Core Capabilities
1. Skill Engine

FlowPilot has 30+ skills out of the box — from writing blog posts to qualifying leads to sending newsletters. Each skill is a registered tool with defined inputs, outputs, and approval requirements. And FlowPilot can create new skills when it encounters novel situations.
2. Persistent Memory
Unlike stateless AI tools, FlowPilot remembers. It stores brand guidelines, user preferences, past decisions, and learned patterns in a structured memory system. Every interaction makes it smarter.
3. Objectives & Goals
You define objectives like "Generate 10 qualified leads per week." FlowPilot tracks progress, adjusts strategies, and reports on results. Goals drive behavior, not manual task lists.
4. Autonomous Heartbeat

On a configurable schedule, FlowPilot runs a reflection cycle. It reviews recent activity, evaluates performance against objectives, identifies opportunities, and plans its next actions. This happens without human input.
5. Signal Automations
When events occur — a form submission, a chat conversation, a new subscriber — FlowPilot reacts instantly. Signals trigger skills: qualify the lead, send a welcome email, enrich the company data.
6. Self-Evolution
FlowPilot can modify its own instructions and create new skills. If it discovers a recurring task that has no skill, it proposes one. If its approach is not working, it adjusts.
Not a Chatbot. An Operator.

Chatbots answer questions. FlowPilot runs operations. It manages your content pipeline, your CRM, your email campaigns, your bookings, and your e-commerce — simultaneously, continuously, autonomously.

meta.description: Deep dive into FlowPilot — the autonomous agent that manages your entire online presence with persistent memory, self-evolving skills, and goal-driven objectives.

blogPosts.title: Self-Hosted AI: Privacy as Competitive Advantage
blogPosts.excerpt: When your AI agent manages content, leads, and customer data, where it runs matters. Self-hosted AI is not just safer — it is strategically superior.

content.text: Your autonomous agent knows everything: your content strategy, your lead pipeline, your customer conversations, your pricing. When that agent runs on someone else's infrastructure, you are trusting them with your entire business intelligence.
The SaaS AI Problem
Your content and prompts train models you do not control
Your customer data flows through third-party servers
Your competitive strategies are visible to the AI provider
Pricing can change — and you cannot leave without losing context
The Self-Hosted Advantage
FlowWink is fully self-hostable. Run FlowPilot on your own infrastructure with your choice of AI provider — OpenAI, Gemini, or a completely private local LLM. Your data never leaves your control.
Private AI Is Not a Compromise

Modern open-source LLMs are remarkably capable. A self-hosted model running on modest hardware can handle content generation, lead qualification, and customer chat with quality that rivals cloud APIs.
GDPR by Architecture

When your CMS, your AI, and your data all live on infrastructure you control, GDPR compliance is architectural, not aspirational. No Data Processing Agreements with AI vendors. No cross-border data transfers. No trust required.
The Strategic Edge
Your AI agent accumulates institutional knowledge over time. That knowledge — stored in persistent memory — becomes a competitive moat. Self-hosting ensures that moat belongs to you, permanently.

meta.description: Learn why self-hosted AI is strategically superior for autonomous CMS. Privacy, control, and competitive advantage through self-hosted FlowPilot.

blogPosts.title: The Autonomous Loop: How FlowPilot Self-Improves
blogPosts.excerpt: On a configurable schedule, FlowPilot runs a heartbeat cycle — reflecting on performance, planning next actions, and evolving its own capabilities. Here is how it works.

content.text: Most AI tools are reactive — you prompt, they respond. FlowPilot is proactive. It operates in a continuous autonomous loop that drives self-improvement without human intervention.
The Six Phases
Phase 1: Heartbeat
On schedule, FlowPilot's heartbeat triggers. It wakes up, gathers context, and begins its reflection cycle.
Phase 2: Reflect
FlowPilot reviews everything that happened since the last heartbeat: content published, leads qualified, emails sent, conversations held, bookings made. It evaluates what worked and what did not.
Phase 3: Plan
Based on active objectives and reflection insights, FlowPilot plans its next actions. "Objective says 4 posts this month. We have published 2. Time to draft the third."
Phase 4: Execute

FlowPilot executes planned actions using its skill engine. Each skill is a well-defined tool — write a blog post, qualify a lead, send a newsletter. Actions that require approval are queued for human review.
Phase 5: Log
Every action is recorded in the activity feed with full context: what was done, why, what data was used, and the outcome. Complete auditability.
Phase 6: Learn
FlowPilot updates its persistent memory with new learnings. Patterns are reinforced. Mistakes are noted. The next heartbeat cycle benefits from accumulated experience.
Approval Gating
Not all actions are autonomous. Skills marked as "requires_approval" queue actions for human review. You see exactly what FlowPilot wants to do and why — then approve, reject, or modify.
The Result
A system that gets better over time. Not because a vendor shipped an update, but because your agent learned from your specific context, your audience, and your objectives.

meta.description: Understanding the Autonomous Loop — how FlowPilot continuously reflects, plans, executes, and self-improves every 12 hours without human intervention.

blogPosts.title: From 4 Products to 1 Agent: Replace Your Stack
blogPosts.excerpt: Most businesses run a CMS, a chatbot, a CRM, and marketing automation as separate products. FlowPilot replaces all four with a single autonomous agent.

content.text: The average business website runs WordPress for content, Intercom for chat, HubSpot for CRM, and Mailchimp for email. Four products, four logins, four bills, and zero integration between them.
The Integration Tax

Every tool boundary is a data gap. A visitor chats on your site, but the CRM does not know. A lead fills out a form, but the email platform does not trigger. You spend hours on Zapier glue, and it still breaks.
One Agent, Six Channels
FlowPilot operates across all channels natively:
Content & Blog — writes, schedules, publishes, optimizes
Visitor Chat — answers questions, qualifies leads, escalates intelligently
CRM — captures, scores, enriches, and manages leads and deals
Email — sends newsletters, drip sequences, booking confirmations
Bookings — schedules appointments, confirms, follows up
E-Commerce — manages products, tracks orders, recommends
The Data Advantage

Because FlowPilot manages everything, it has complete context. When a visitor asks about pricing in chat, FlowPilot knows they downloaded your whitepaper last week and viewed the enterprise page twice. That context makes every interaction smarter.
The Cost Equation
Replace four SaaS subscriptions with one self-hosted platform:
WordPress + plugins: €200-500/month
Intercom or Drift: €100-300/month
HubSpot CRM: €100-800/month
Mailchimp or ConvertKit: €50-200/month
Total: €450-1,800/month → FlowWink on a €20/month VPS
Migration Is Simpler Than You Think

Start by deploying FlowWink alongside your existing stack. Let FlowPilot handle one channel first — typically content or chat. As confidence grows, consolidate. Within 90 days, you can run everything from a single agent.

meta.description: Learn how FlowPilot replaces your CMS, chatbot, CRM, and marketing automation with a single autonomous agent. Reduce costs and eliminate integration headaches.

blogPosts.title: Skill Engine: How FlowPilot Learns New Skills
blogPosts.excerpt: FlowPilot ships with 30+ skills and can create new ones autonomously. Here is how the skill engine works — registration, routing, approval, and self-evolution.

content.text: Traditional software has features. FlowPilot has skills — modular, registered capabilities that it can invoke, chain, and even create on its own.
What Is a Skill?

A skill is a database-registered tool defined in OpenAI function-calling format. Each skill has: a name and description, input parameters, an execution handler, a scope (internal, external, or both), and an approval requirement flag.
Built-In Skills
FlowPilot ships with skills for every module:
Content: create_page, publish_page, write_blog_post
CRM: qualify_lead, enrich_company, create_deal
Email: send_newsletter, add_subscriber
Booking: check_availability, confirm_booking
Knowledge: search_kb, update_article
System: soul_update, skill_instruct, reflect
Scope and Security

Skills have scope — "internal" skills are only available to admin operators, "external" skills can be invoked by visitor chat. This prevents visitors from triggering administrative actions while allowing FlowPilot to serve both audiences.
Approval Gating

Skills marked as "requires_approval" queue their output for human review before execution. Publishing a blog post? FlowPilot writes and prepares it, then waits for your approval. Qualifying a lead? Happens instantly — no approval needed.
Self-Creating Skills

When FlowPilot encounters a task that no existing skill covers, it can propose a new skill using the "skill_instruct" tool. It defines the skill's parameters, handler, and scope — then awaits admin approval to register it.
The Handler Router
Each skill's handler field determines where execution happens:
edge: — executes a Supabase Edge Function
module: — triggers internal module logic
db: — performs a database operation
webhook: — calls an external service
Why Skills Beat Features

Features are static — they exist or they do not. Skills are dynamic, composable, and evolvable. FlowPilot can chain skills, create new ones, and optimize existing ones. Your CMS literally becomes more capable over time.

meta.description: Deep dive into FlowPilot's skill engine — how skills are registered, routed, approved, and self-created. Understand the architecture that makes autonomous CMS possible.

blogPosts.title: Objectives vs Automations: Two Modes of Autonomous Operation
blogPosts.excerpt: FlowPilot operates in two cadences: one-time objectives with tracked progress, and recurring automations on cron schedules. Understanding when to use each is key to effective autonomous operations.
blogPosts.featured_image_alt: Strategic planning board with goals and recurring tasks

content.text: FlowPilot is not a single-mode system. It operates in two distinct cadences, each designed for a different kind of work. Understanding the difference is the key to getting the most out of your autonomous agent.
Objectives: Finite Goals With Tracked Progress

An objective is a high-level, one-time goal. "Research competitor positioning and draft a strategy memo." "Publish a 5-part blog series on AI in healthcare." Each objective has a clear definition of done.
How Objectives Work
You define the goal and success criteria
FlowPilot decomposes it into actionable steps
Progress is tracked via structured JSONB fields
Each step is logged in the activity feed
The objective completes when all criteria are met
Objectives live in the agent_objectives table with status tracking (active, completed, paused, failed) and a progress field that FlowPilot updates as it works.
Automations: Recurring Routines on Schedule

Automations are the opposite — they never "complete." They run on a schedule, executing a specific skill at defined intervals. Weekly competitor monitoring. Daily content research. Hourly lead scoring.
How Automations Work
Define a cron schedule (e.g., every Monday at 9 AM)
Select the skill to execute
Configure skill arguments
FlowPilot executes on schedule, logs results
Automations are configured in Admin → Engine Room → Automations. Each automation has a trigger type, skill reference, and execution history.
When to Use Which

Use objectives for finite, strategic work — projects with a clear end state. Use automations for ongoing operational routines that should happen regardless of objectives. The combination gives FlowPilot both strategic direction and operational rhythm.
They Work Together

An objective might discover that weekly competitor monitoring would be valuable. FlowPilot can propose creating an automation as part of completing that objective. Strategic thinking feeds operational execution.

meta.description: Learn the difference between FlowPilot objectives and automations — two complementary modes of autonomous operation for strategic goals and recurring routines.

blogPosts.title: Agent-to-Agent: How FlowPilot Talks to Other AI Agents
blogPosts.excerpt: The agentic web is not one agent per company — it is agents collaborating across boundaries. FlowPilot supports A2A peer connections with token-authenticated communication.
blogPosts.featured_image_alt: Network of connected nodes representing agent-to-agent communication

content.text: The future of the web is not humans talking to websites. It is agents talking to agents. FlowPilot is built for this future with native Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol support.
What Is A2A?

A2A is a peer-to-peer communication protocol that allows AI agents to discover capabilities, exchange tasks, and collaborate — without human intermediation. Think of it as APIs, but for autonomous agents.
How FlowPilot Implements A2A
Peer Registry

Each A2A connection is a registered peer in the a2a_peers table. Peers have a name, URL endpoint, capabilities manifest, and authentication tokens. FlowPilot can both send and receive tasks from peers.
Token Authentication
Security is built in. Each peer connection uses separate inbound and outbound tokens. Inbound tokens are stored as hashes. Outbound tokens are generated automatically. No shared secrets.
Capability Discovery
When a peer connects, it shares its capabilities — what skills it can execute, what data it can provide. FlowPilot can query peer capabilities before sending tasks.
Use Cases
A content agent at Company A requests market research from an analyst agent at Company B
A sales agent sends qualified leads to a partner's booking agent
A monitoring agent alerts FlowPilot about brand mentions for autonomous response
Multiple FlowWink instances collaborate on multi-site content strategies
Activity Tracking
Every A2A interaction is logged in a2a_activity with direction (inbound/outbound), payload, status, and duration. Full auditability for cross-agent operations.
The Agentic Web

A2A transforms your website from an isolated node into a participant in a network of intelligent agents. FlowPilot does not just manage your site — it negotiates, collaborates, and coordinates with other agents across the internet.

meta.description: Explore FlowPilot's Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol — how autonomous agents discover capabilities, authenticate, and collaborate across organizational boundaries.

blogPosts.title: Content Authority: FlowPilot and Thought Leadership

Social Post Generation

blogPosts.title: The 7-Step FlowAgent Loop Explained
blogPosts.excerpt: FlowPilot's reasoning engine has evolved into a 7-step autonomous loop. Understanding each phase reveals how an AI agent maintains, improves, and operates your entire digital presence.
blogPosts.featured_image_alt: Circular process diagram representing autonomous loop

content.text: FlowPilot's autonomous engine — internally called FlowAgent — has matured from a simple heartbeat into a sophisticated 7-step reasoning loop. Each step serves a distinct purpose in maintaining and advancing your digital operations.
Step 1: Self-Heal

Before doing anything new, FlowPilot checks system health. Are all edge functions deployed? Are API keys valid? Are there failed skills that need retry? Self-healing ensures the foundation is stable before advancing.
Step 2: Propose

FlowPilot evaluates the current state against objectives and proposes what to do next. This is strategic thinking — not just task execution. It considers priorities, resource constraints, and past learnings.
Step 3: Plan

Proposals become plans. FlowPilot decomposes high-level proposals into specific skill calls with defined parameters. A plan to "publish a blog post about AI trends" becomes: research_topic → write_blog_post → optimize_seo → queue_for_approval.
Step 4: Advance
Plans execute. FlowPilot runs skills through the unified agent-execute edge function, which handles tool routing, approval gating, and audit logging. Each skill execution is atomic and traceable.
Step 5: Automate
After advancing, FlowPilot checks for patterns that should become automations. If it has been manually performing the same research every week, it proposes a recurring automation to handle it.
Step 6: Reflect
FlowPilot reviews what happened during this cycle. What succeeded? What failed? What took longer than expected? Reflection is not just logging — it is active evaluation that shapes future decisions.
Step 7: Remember

Learnings from reflection are stored in persistent memory. Brand voice insights, successful content patterns, lead scoring calibrations — all persist across sessions. The agent gets smarter with every cycle.
The Shared Reasoning Engine

The agent-reason shared module powers this loop for both interactive tasks (Operate mode) and autonomous tasks (Heartbeat). Whether FlowPilot is responding to a chat message or running its scheduled cycle, the same reasoning engine ensures consistent, intelligent decision-making.

meta.description: Deep dive into the 7-step FlowAgent loop — Self-Heal, Propose, Plan, Advance, Automate, Reflect, Remember — the reasoning engine behind autonomous CMS operations.

blogPosts.title: Module Registry: Orchestration Behind FlowWink
blogPosts.excerpt: FlowWink's Module Registry API organizes 15+ platform features into versioned, Zod-validated modules that FlowPilot can orchestrate. Here is how data flows between modules.

content.text: FlowWink is not a monolith with bolted-on features. It is an orchestrated collection of modules — each with a versioned API contract, Zod-validated schemas, and well-defined data flow patterns.
What Is the Module Registry?

The Module Registry (src/lib/modules/) is a centralized coordinator for all platform features. Each module defines its capabilities, input/output schemas, and publishing logic. FlowPilot uses the registry to understand what operations are available and how to invoke them correctly.
Available Modules
Blog — create, publish, and manage blog posts
Pages — create and manage site pages
Knowledge Base — create KB categories and articles
Newsletter — manage subscribers and send campaigns
CRM — leads, deals, and companies
Products & Orders — e-commerce operations
Booking — services, availability, and appointments
Forms — submission handling and lead routing
Media — file management and optimization
Global Blocks — site-wide header, footer, popup
Webinars — event management and registration
Sales Intelligence — lead enrichment and scoring
Resume Matcher — talent matching capabilities
The API Contract

Each module follows a strict contract: versioned ID, Zod input schema, Zod output schema, capabilities list, and a publish() method. This means FlowPilot can validate inputs before execution and guarantee output structure — no surprises.
Data Flow Between Modules

Modules communicate through signals and shared data. When a form submission creates a lead (Forms → CRM), the signal system triggers the appropriate module operations. When a blog post is published (Blog → Newsletter), the content flows automatically to subscribers.
Why This Architecture Matters

For FlowPilot, the module registry is its toolkit. Each module is a well-defined capability with predictable behavior. This architecture enables reliable autonomous operations — FlowPilot knows exactly what each module can do and how to invoke it safely.

meta.description: Explore FlowWink's Module Registry API — how 15+ platform features are organized into versioned, validated modules that FlowPilot orchestrates for autonomous operations.

blogPosts.title: Google A2A Protocol and the Rise of the Agentic Web
blogPosts.excerpt: Google's Agent-to-Agent protocol is setting the standard for how autonomous agents discover, authenticate, and collaborate. Here is what it means for FlowPilot and the future of the web.
blogPosts.featured_image_alt: Interconnected digital network representing agent collaboration

content.text: In April 2025, Google released the Agent-to-Agent (A2A) protocol — an open standard for AI agents to discover each other, negotiate capabilities, and exchange tasks across organizational boundaries. This is not a minor API update. It is the TCP/IP moment for autonomous systems.
What Is Google A2A?

A2A defines three primitives: Agent Cards (capability discovery), Task Objects (structured work units), and Streaming Updates (real-time progress). Any agent that speaks A2A can collaborate with any other — regardless of vendor, framework, or hosting.
Why This Matters for FlowPilot
FlowPilot already has native A2A support through its peer registry. With Google formalizing the protocol, the ecosystem of compatible agents is about to explode:
Partner agencies can connect their FlowPilot instances for cross-site content strategies
Specialized research agents can feed market intelligence directly into your CRM
Sales agents from partner companies can exchange qualified leads automatically
Monitoring agents can alert FlowPilot about brand mentions for autonomous response
From Assistant to Consultant

The agentic web transforms FlowPilot from an assistant that executes your commands into a consultant that proactively collaborates. When FlowPilot can query a market research agent, cross-reference with a competitor analysis agent, and synthesize findings — it does not just manage your website. It advises your business.
The Ecosystem Will Grow Exponentially

Today, most organizations run isolated AI tools. A2A changes the economics: every new agent in the network increases the value for all participants. FlowPilot on your infrastructure is not just a CMS operator — it is your node in the emerging agentic web.
Getting Started

FlowPilot's A2A implementation is ready today. Connect peers in Admin → Agent Hub → A2A Peers. Define capabilities, exchange tokens, and start collaborating. As the ecosystem matures, your FlowPilot instance is already a first-class citizen.

meta.description: How Google's A2A protocol is shaping the agentic web and what it means for FlowPilot — from isolated assistant to networked consultant.

blogPosts.title: Running AI Models Locally: A Practical Guide
blogPosts.excerpt: Open-source LLMs have reached a tipping point. Models like Llama, Mistral, and Gemma can run on modest hardware with quality that rivals cloud APIs. Here is how to set it up with FlowWink.
blogPosts.featured_image_alt: Server hardware running local AI models

content.text: The narrative that AI requires massive cloud infrastructure is outdated. In 2025, a €500 GPU can run models that generate production-quality content, qualify leads, and hold nuanced customer conversations — all without a single byte leaving your network.
The Local AI Landscape in 2025
The open-source AI ecosystem has matured dramatically:
Llama 3.1 (Meta) — 8B to 405B parameters, excellent reasoning and multilingual support
Mistral/Mixtral — fast inference, strong at structured tasks like JSON generation
Gemma 2 (Google) — optimized for edge deployment, surprisingly capable at 9B
Phi-3 (Microsoft) — tiny models (3.8B) that punch well above their weight
Qwen 2.5 — strong coding and analytical capabilities
Why Local Matters
Zero Data Leakage

When FlowPilot processes customer conversations, qualifies leads, and drafts content using a local model, no data leaves your infrastructure. GDPR compliance becomes architectural fact, not a legal promise.
Predictable Costs

Cloud AI bills are unpredictable. A busy month with heavy chat traffic can spike costs. Local inference has a fixed cost — the hardware. After the initial investment, every additional query is essentially free.
No Vendor Lock-In
When your AI runs locally, you own the entire stack. No API deprecations, no surprise pricing changes, no terms of service updates that affect your operations.
FlowWink's Local AI Integration

FlowPilot supports local AI through the "local" provider in the chat-completion edge function. Point it at any OpenAI-compatible API endpoint — Ollama, llama.cpp, vLLM, or LocalAI — and FlowPilot uses your local model for all operations.
Recommended Setup
Ollama for easy model management and serving
A single NVIDIA RTX 4060 (8GB) handles 7-8B models comfortably
For larger models (70B+), consider RTX 4090 or A100
CPU-only inference works for low-traffic sites (slower but viable)
The Hybrid Approach

Many FlowWink deployments use a hybrid model: local AI for day-to-day operations (chat, lead qualification, content drafts) and cloud APIs (OpenAI, Gemini) for complex reasoning tasks. FlowPilot routes automatically based on task complexity.

meta.description: Practical guide to running AI models locally with FlowWink. Cover hardware requirements, model selection, and the privacy advantages of local inference.

blogPosts.title: The Agentic Web Is Here: What It Means for Your Business
blogPosts.excerpt: We are entering the age of autonomous agents. Websites that operate themselves, agents that collaborate, and AI that acts — not just answers. Here is what forward-thinking businesses need to know.
blogPosts.featured_image_alt: Abstract visualization of connected autonomous agents

content.text: The web is undergoing its biggest transformation since mobile. Not in how it looks — in how it operates. We are moving from a web of pages to a web of agents. And the businesses that understand this shift will have an extraordinary advantage.
From Reactive to Autonomous

Traditional websites wait. They wait for visitors to click, for admins to update content, for marketers to send emails. An agentic website operates. It publishes content, qualifies leads, sends campaigns, and optimizes itself — continuously, autonomously.
The Three Waves of the Agentic Web
Wave 1: Single-Agent Autonomy (Now)
Individual agents managing individual properties. FlowPilot operating your website, qualifying your leads, publishing your content. This is where most early adopters are today.
Wave 2: Agent Collaboration (2025-2026)

Agents talking to agents via protocols like Google A2A. Your FlowPilot negotiating with a partner's agent to exchange leads, coordinate campaigns, or share market intelligence. The network effect begins.
Wave 3: Agent Economies (2027+)

Agents transacting autonomously. Your agent hiring a specialized research agent for a one-time analysis, paying in tokens, and integrating the results — all without human intervention. Micro-economies of specialized capabilities.
What This Means for Your Business
Your website is no longer a brochure — it is an autonomous operator
Your CRM is not a database — it is an intelligence system
Your content strategy is not a calendar — it is an objective for an agent
Your competitive advantage is not just your product — it is how effectively your agents operate
The Privacy Imperative

In an agentic world, your agent knows everything about your business. Where that agent runs, who controls its memory, and what data it shares with other agents — these become existential strategic decisions. Self-hosted agents on private infrastructure are not a preference. They are a necessity.
Starting Today

You do not need to wait for the full agentic web to arrive. FlowPilot gives you Wave 1 autonomy today, with Wave 2 A2A collaboration already built in. Start with a single objective — "publish 4 blog posts this month" — and experience what autonomous operations feel like.

meta.description: The agentic web is transforming how businesses operate online. Understand the three waves of agent autonomy and why self-hosted AI agents are the future.

blogPosts.title: MCP and Tool Use: How FlowPilot Stays Agnostic
blogPosts.excerpt: OpenAI function calling, Anthropic MCP, Google A2A — the AI ecosystem is fragmenting into competing standards. FlowPilot's skill engine is designed to work with all of them.
blogPosts.featured_image_alt: Code on screen representing multiple AI frameworks

content.text: The AI tool-use landscape is evolving fast. OpenAI has function calling. Anthropic introduced Model Context Protocol (MCP). Google launched A2A. Each approach has merits — and FlowPilot is designed to work with all of them.
The Standards Landscape
OpenAI Function Calling

The original standard. Define tools as JSON schemas, the model decides when to call them. FlowPilot's skill engine uses this format as its native representation — every skill is an OpenAI-compatible function definition.
Anthropic MCP (Model Context Protocol)

MCP takes a different approach: rather than the model calling functions, MCP servers expose resources and tools that any compatible client can discover and invoke. It is more about standardizing the bridge between AI and data sources.
Google A2A (Agent-to-Agent)

A2A operates at a higher level — agent-to-agent collaboration. While function calling and MCP are about tool use within an agent, A2A is about coordination between agents. These are complementary, not competing.
FlowPilot's Approach: Skill as Abstraction
FlowPilot's skill engine is deliberately provider-agnostic. A skill defines what it does, not how it is invoked. The handler router translates between skill definitions and the appropriate protocol:
OpenAI models receive skills as function-calling tool definitions
Local models (via Ollama) use the same OpenAI-compatible format
A2A peers receive skill capabilities as Agent Card manifests
Future MCP integration will expose skills as MCP tool resources
Why This Matters

The AI industry moves fast. Last year's dominant framework is this year's legacy. By abstracting skills from their invocation protocol, FlowPilot protects your investment. Your skills, your memory, your objectives — all survive framework changes.
The Pragmatic Path

Today, use OpenAI or Gemini function calling. Tomorrow, add MCP servers for richer data integration. Next month, connect A2A peers for agent collaboration. FlowPilot's architecture supports all of these without rewriting a single skill.

meta.description: How FlowPilot stays framework-agnostic across OpenAI function calling, Anthropic MCP, and Google A2A — protecting your investment as AI standards evolve.

articles.title: What is FlowWink?
articles.question: What is FlowWink and what can I do with it?
articles.answer_text: FlowWink is the first autonomous Business Operating System (BOS). Instead of managing your business manually across separate tools, you set objectives and FlowPilot — an autonomous agent — operates your entire online presence. It writes content, qualifies leads, sends campaigns, books meetings, and learns from every interaction. FlowWink replaces your CMS, CRM, ERP, and marketing stack — with a single self-hosted platform.

articles.title: What is FlowPilot?
articles.question: What is FlowPilot and how does it work?
articles.answer_text: FlowPilot is an autonomous AI agent inspired by the OpenClaw framework. It has six core capabilities: Skill Engine (20+ registered tools), Persistent Memory (learns and remembers), Objectives (goal-driven operations), Autonomous Heartbeat (12h reflection cycles), Signal Automations (event-driven reactions), and Self-Evolution (creates new skills and updates its own instructions). You set objectives. FlowPilot executes.

articles.title: Setting Your First Objective
articles.question: How do I set objectives for FlowPilot?
articles.answer_text: Navigate to Admin → FlowPilot → Objectives. Click "New Objective" and describe your goal, e.g., "Publish 4 blog posts per month" or "Qualify all inbound leads within 5 minutes." Define success criteria and any constraints. FlowPilot breaks objectives into actions and tracks progress autonomously. Review progress in the Activity Feed.

articles.title: Understanding User Roles
articles.question: What are the different user roles in FlowWink?
articles.answer_text: FlowWink has three roles: Writer – Can create and edit their own drafts, submit for review. Approver – Can review content, approve/reject submissions, publish content. Admin – Full access to all features including FlowPilot configuration, skills, objectives, and site settings. FlowPilot itself operates as a system-level agent with approval gating for sensitive actions.

articles.title: The Activity Feed
articles.question: How do I see what FlowPilot is doing?
articles.answer_text: The Activity Feed (Admin → FlowPilot → Activity) shows every action FlowPilot takes — content drafted, leads qualified, emails sent, skills executed. Each entry includes what was done, why, input/output data, and duration. Actions requiring approval appear with Approve/Reject buttons. This is your command center for reviewing autonomous operations.

kbCategories.name: FlowPilot Skills
kbCategories.description: Understand and manage FlowPilot's autonomous capabilities.

articles.title: Skills Overview
articles.question: What are FlowPilot skills?
articles.answer_text: Skills are FlowPilot's registered capabilities — modular tools defined in OpenAI function-calling format. Each skill has a name, description, input parameters, execution handler, scope (internal/external), and an approval requirement flag. FlowPilot ships with 30+ skills and can create new ones autonomously.

articles.title: Built-in Skills
articles.question: What skills does FlowPilot have out of the box?
articles.answer_text: Built-in skills include: Content (create_page, publish_page, write_blog_post), CRM (qualify_lead, enrich_company, create_deal), Email (send_newsletter, add_subscriber), Booking (check_availability, confirm_booking), Knowledge (search_kb, update_article), and System (soul_update, skill_instruct, reflect). Enable or disable skills in Admin → FlowPilot → Skills.

articles.title: Skill Scopes
articles.question: What are internal and external skill scopes?
articles.answer_text: Scope controls who can trigger a skill. "Internal" skills are only available to FlowPilot operating as an admin assistant. "External" skills can be invoked by the visitor-facing chat. Skills can also be set to "both." This prevents visitors from triggering administrative actions while allowing FlowPilot to serve both audiences safely.

articles.title: Approval Gating
articles.question: How does approval gating work for FlowPilot actions?
articles.answer_text: Skills marked as "requires_approval" queue their output for human review before execution. For example, publishing a blog post gets queued — FlowPilot writes and prepares it, then waits for your approval. Lead qualification happens instantly, no approval needed. Configure approval requirements per skill in Admin → FlowPilot → Skills.

articles.title: Creating Custom Skills
articles.question: Can FlowPilot create new skills?
articles.answer_text: Yes. When FlowPilot encounters a task no existing skill covers, it can propose a new one using the "skill_instruct" tool. It defines the skill's parameters, handler, and scope — then awaits admin approval to register it. You can also create skills manually in Admin → FlowPilot → Skills → New Skill.

kbCategories.name: Autonomous Operations
kbCategories.description: How FlowPilot manages content, leads, campaigns, and more without manual intervention.

articles.title: The Autonomous Loop
articles.question: What is the Autonomous Loop?
articles.answer_text: FlowPilot operates in a continuous cycle: Heartbeat (triggers every 12h) → Reflect (reviews recent activity) → Plan (decides next actions based on objectives) → Execute (runs skills) → Log (records everything) → Learn (updates persistent memory). This loop runs without human intervention, making your website truly self-operating.

articles.title: Persistent Memory
articles.question: How does FlowPilot remember things?
articles.answer_text: FlowPilot stores information in structured persistent memory with categories: brand (guidelines, tone), preferences (learned behaviors), context (recent decisions), and skills (execution patterns). Memory persists across sessions, making FlowPilot smarter over time. View memory entries in Admin → FlowPilot → Memory.

articles.title: Signal Automations
articles.question: What are signal automations?
articles.answer_text: Signals are event-driven triggers that activate FlowPilot skills instantly. When events occur — form submission, chat message, new subscriber, booking request — signals trigger appropriate skills: qualify the lead, send a welcome email, enrich company data. Configure signals in Admin → FlowPilot → Automations.

articles.title: Self-Evolution
articles.question: Can FlowPilot modify itself?
articles.answer_text: Yes. FlowPilot can update its own instructions (soul_update) and propose new skills (skill_instruct). If its approach is not producing results, it adjusts. If it discovers a recurring task with no skill, it proposes one. All self-modifications are logged and require admin approval.

articles.title: Content Autopilot
articles.question: How does FlowPilot handle content creation?
articles.answer_text: Set an objective like "Publish 4 blog posts per month about AI in healthcare." FlowPilot will: research topics using your existing content, draft posts matching your brand voice (from persistent memory), create SEO-optimized titles and meta descriptions, queue for your approval, and publish on schedule. It learns from your edits to improve future drafts.

articles.title: Lead Qualification Autopilot
articles.question: How does FlowPilot qualify leads automatically?
articles.answer_text: When a lead arrives via form, chat, or newsletter signup, FlowPilot: scores based on behavior and profile, enriches company data from the domain, generates an AI qualification summary, assigns to the appropriate team member, and creates a deal if the score exceeds threshold. All within seconds, 24/7.

kbCategories.name: Page Editing
kbCategories.description: Master the block editor and create beautiful pages — or let FlowPilot create them for you.

articles.title: Block Types Overview
articles.question: What types of blocks are available in FlowWink?
articles.answer_text: FlowWink offers 43+ block types: Layout blocks (Hero, Two Column, Separator), Content blocks (Text, Image, Gallery, Video), Interactive blocks (Form, Contact, Accordion, Tabs, Marquee, Table, Embed), Marketing blocks (CTA, Pricing, Testimonials, Stats, Countdown, Progress, Badge, Social Proof), Navigation blocks (Header, Footer, Link Grid, Announcement Bar), Notification blocks (Popup, Floating CTA, Notification Toast), and Special blocks (Products, Booking, Chat, Newsletter, KB blocks). FlowPilot can create and edit pages using these blocks autonomously.

articles.title: Publishing Workflow
articles.question: How does the publishing workflow work?
articles.answer_text: Content flows through Draft → Reviewing → Published → Archived. Writers and FlowPilot create drafts. Approvers review and authorize. FlowPilot can manage this entire pipeline autonomously — drafting content, submitting for review, and publishing approved content on schedule.

articles.title: Version History
articles.question: How does version history work?
articles.answer_text: Every save creates a recoverable version — whether by a human editor or FlowPilot. View all versions, compare changes, and restore any previous version. This provides a complete audit trail of both manual and autonomous content changes.

articles.title: AI Text Assistant
articles.question: How do I use the AI text generation in the editor?
articles.answer_text: The AI Text Assistant (Cmd+J) helps write content faster: generate from keywords, improve grammar, expand or shorten content, translate, and summarize. It uses your brand context from FlowPilot's persistent memory for consistent voice. Available in every text editor across the platform.

articles.title: SEO Settings
articles.question: How do I configure SEO for my pages?
articles.answer_text: Each page has SEO settings: meta title, description, and Open Graph image. FlowWink auto-generates structured data, sitemaps, and LLMs.txt for AI search engines. FlowPilot can optimize SEO automatically when creating content, applying best practices from persistent memory.

kbCategories.name: Troubleshooting
kbCategories.description: Common issues and how to resolve them.

articles.title: FlowPilot Not Responding
articles.question: FlowPilot is not responding or executing skills — what do I do?
articles.answer_text: Check: 1) AI provider is configured and tested in Admin → Settings → Integrations, 2) Skills are enabled in Admin → FlowPilot → Skills, 3) Edge functions are deployed (check deployment status), 4) API keys have not expired, 5) Browser console (F12) for error messages. If using a local LLM, verify the endpoint is accessible.

articles.title: Page Not Saving
articles.question: Why is my page not saving?
articles.answer_text: If your page is not saving: 1) Check Internet Connection, 2) Refresh the page, 3) Check browser console (F12) for errors, 4) Clear browser cache, 5) Try another browser. Auto-save runs continuously, so recent changes should be preserved even if you lose connection briefly.

articles.title: Approval Queue Issues
articles.question: FlowPilot actions are stuck in the approval queue — what should I do?
articles.answer_text: Check Admin → FlowPilot → Activity for pending approvals. Each entry shows what FlowPilot wants to do and why. Approve or reject actions. If too many actions require approval, consider adjusting skill approval settings — mark routine skills as auto-approved to reduce queue friction.

articles.title: Login Issues
articles.question: I cannot log in to the admin panel
articles.answer_text: Verify email and password are correct. Check caps lock. Try "Forgot Password" to reset. If using a self-hosted instance, verify the Supabase connection is active. Contact your administrator to verify your account is active.

kbCategories.name: AI Configuration
kbCategories.description: Configure AI providers that power FlowPilot and the visitor chat.

articles.title: AI Providers Overview
articles.question: What AI providers does FlowWink support?
articles.answer_text: FlowWink supports: 1) OpenAI (GPT models — recommended for most use cases), 2) Google Gemini (multimodal, large context), 3) Local LLM (self-hosted, OpenAI-compatible — for complete privacy), 4) N8N Webhook (custom AI workflows). Configure in Admin → Settings → Integrations. The same provider powers both FlowPilot autonomous operations and the visitor-facing chat.

articles.title: Setting Up Local LLM
articles.question: How do I connect my own LLM for complete privacy?
articles.answer_text: For complete data sovereignty: 1) Set up an OpenAI-compatible endpoint (Ollama, LM Studio, vLLM). 2) In Integrations, enable Local LLM. 3) Enter your endpoint URL (e.g., http://localhost:11434/v1). 4) Specify the model name. 5) Click Test Connection. Your content, leads, and customer data never leave your infrastructure. FlowPilot operates with the same capabilities regardless of provider.

articles.title: Chat System Prompt
articles.question: How do I customize FlowPilot's personality and behavior?
articles.answer_text: In Admin → Chat Settings, customize the system prompt that defines FlowPilot's personality for visitor-facing chat. Set tone, boundaries, and knowledge scope. FlowPilot's internal personality evolves through the soul_update mechanism — it refines its own instructions based on accumulated experience.

articles.title: N8N AI Agent Integration
articles.question: How do I connect FlowWink to an N8N AI Agent?
articles.answer_text: Create an N8N workflow with a Chat Trigger or Webhook node. Configure AI Agent with tools. Copy the webhook URL to FlowWink's N8N integration settings. This extends FlowPilot's capabilities with custom workflows — connecting to external services, databases, or specialized AI models.

kbCategories.name: Channels FlowPilot Manages
kbCategories.description: Content, CRM, email, bookings, e-commerce — all managed by one agent.

articles.title: Blog & Content
articles.question: How does FlowPilot manage blog and content?
articles.answer_text: FlowPilot writes, edits, schedules, and publishes blog posts and pages based on your objectives. It uses persistent memory to maintain brand voice, applies SEO best practices, and learns from your editorial feedback. Features: categories, tags, author profiles, RSS feed, featured posts, reading time, and rich text editing with 43+ block types.

articles.title: CRM & Lead Management
articles.question: How does FlowPilot manage leads and CRM?
articles.answer_text: FlowPilot captures leads from forms, chat, and newsletter signups. It scores leads based on behavior, enriches company data from domains, generates AI qualification summaries, and manages deals through a Kanban pipeline (Proposal → Negotiation → Won/Lost). All 24/7 with instant response times.

articles.title: Newsletter & Email
articles.question: How does FlowPilot handle email campaigns?
articles.answer_text: FlowPilot manages subscriber lists, writes campaign content, and sends newsletters with open/click tracking. Signal automations trigger welcome sequences when new subscribers join. GDPR-compliant with double opt-in and one-click unsubscribe. Uses Resend for delivery.

articles.title: Booking & Scheduling
articles.question: How does FlowPilot manage bookings?
articles.answer_text: Visitors book appointments directly on your site. FlowPilot manages services, availability, confirmations, and follow-ups. It sends reminder emails, creates leads from bookings, and can reschedule or cancel when triggered by signals.

articles.title: E-Commerce & Products
articles.question: How does FlowPilot handle e-commerce?
articles.answer_text: FlowPilot manages product catalogs, processes orders via Stripe checkout, sends order confirmations, and tracks fulfillment. It can recommend products based on visitor behavior and trigger follow-up campaigns for completed purchases.

articles.title: Visitor Chat
articles.question: How does FlowPilot handle visitor conversations?
articles.answer_text: FlowPilot powers an intelligent chat widget using Context Augmented Generation. It searches your knowledge base, pages, and blog posts to provide accurate answers. Complex questions are escalated to human agents. Chat conversations are logged, sentiment-analyzed, and can trigger CRM actions.

kbCategories.name: Global Blocks & Branding
kbCategories.description: Manage site-wide elements, themes, and visual identity.

articles.title: Global Blocks Overview
articles.question: What are Global Blocks?
articles.answer_text: Global Blocks are reusable elements across all pages: Header (navigation), Footer (links, branding), and Popup (modals for announcements or lead capture). Edit once, update everywhere. Access via Admin → Global Blocks.

articles.title: Branding Settings
articles.question: How do I customize my site's look and feel?
articles.answer_text: Go to Admin → Branding to configure site name, logo, favicon, primary color, fonts, and dark mode. Changes apply site-wide instantly. FlowPilot uses branding settings to maintain consistent visual identity when creating content or pages autonomously.

articles.title: Dark Mode
articles.question: Does FlowWink support dark mode?
articles.answer_text: Yes. Enable the theme toggle in the header to let visitors switch between light and dark modes. The site adapts colors, images, and contrast automatically. Set a default theme preference in branding settings.

articles.title: Brand Guide AI
articles.question: What is Brand Guide AI?
articles.answer_text: Brand Guide AI analyzes your website and extracts your brand identity — colors, fonts, tone, visual style. It feeds these into FlowPilot's persistent memory so all autonomous content matches your brand. Access from the Branding settings page.

kbCategories.name: Self-Hosting & Deployment
kbCategories.description: Run FlowWink and FlowPilot on your own infrastructure with Docker.

articles.title: Self-Hosting Overview
articles.question: Can I self-host FlowWink?
articles.answer_text: Yes. FlowWink is fully self-hostable using Docker. Your autonomous agent, your data, your AI — all on infrastructure you control. Benefits: complete data sovereignty, private LLM support, custom domain, no per-seat licensing, and no vendor lock-in. FlowPilot operates identically whether self-hosted or cloud-deployed.

articles.title: Docker Quick Start
articles.question: How do I deploy FlowWink with Docker?
articles.answer_text: Quick start: 1) Create docker-compose.yml with the FlowWink image, 2) Set environment variables for Supabase connection, 3) Run docker-compose up -d, 4) Access at localhost:8080, 5) Configure your AI provider, 6) Set your first FlowPilot objective. See docs/DOCKER-QUICKSTART.md for complete instructions.

articles.title: Environment Variables
articles.question: What environment variables do I need?
articles.answer_text: Required: VITE_SUPABASE_URL, VITE_SUPABASE_PUBLISHABLE_KEY. Optional: API keys for OpenAI, Gemini, Resend (email), Unsplash, Firecrawl. For private LLM: configure endpoint URL in integrations. All secrets are stored securely. FlowPilot works with any configured AI provider.

articles.title: Connecting Your Domain
articles.question: How do I connect my custom domain?
articles.answer_text: Configure your reverse proxy (nginx, Caddy, Traefik) to point to the FlowWink container. Add SSL with Let's Encrypt. Update your branding settings with the production domain for correct canonical URLs and sitemap generation.

articles.title: Upgrading FlowWink
articles.question: How do I upgrade to a new version?
articles.answer_text: Pull the latest Docker image and restart your container. Database migrations run automatically on startup. FlowPilot's persistent memory and skills are preserved across upgrades. Always backup your database before major upgrades. Check CHANGELOG.md for breaking changes.

kbCategories.name: Security & Privacy
kbCategories.description: Data sovereignty, GDPR compliance, and audit trails in an autonomous system.

articles.title: Security Model
articles.question: How does FlowWink handle security with an autonomous agent?
articles.answer_text: FlowPilot respects all Row-Level Security policies and authentication constraints. AI-triggered actions follow the same security rules as manual operations. Skill scopes prevent unauthorized access. Approval gating adds human oversight for sensitive actions. Every action is logged in the audit trail.

articles.title: GDPR Compliance
articles.question: Is FlowWink GDPR compliant?
articles.answer_text: When self-hosted with a private LLM, FlowWink is GDPR compliant by architecture — not policy. Your data, your AI, your infrastructure. No cross-border transfers. No third-party DPAs needed. Newsletter includes double opt-in and one-click unsubscribe. Complete data export available.

articles.title: Audit Trail
articles.question: Is there a complete audit trail?
articles.answer_text: Yes. Every action — manual or autonomous — is logged: who did it (user or FlowPilot), what was done, when, and why. View audit logs in Admin → Settings → Audit Log. This includes content changes, lead actions, email sends, skill executions, and self-evolution events.

articles.title: Private AI Setup
articles.question: How do I ensure my AI is completely private?
articles.answer_text: Deploy FlowWink with Docker on your infrastructure. Configure a Local LLM (Ollama, LM Studio, vLLM) instead of cloud AI providers. Your prompts, content, lead data, and customer conversations never leave your network. FlowPilot's persistent memory is stored in your own database.

kbCategories.name: Content Best Practices
kbCategories.description: Tips for effective content — whether written by you or FlowPilot.

articles.title: SEO Optimization
articles.question: How does FlowWink handle SEO?
articles.answer_text: Automatic SEO features: XML sitemap, RSS feed, canonical URLs, mobile-responsive design, structured data, and LLMs.txt for AI search engines. FlowPilot applies SEO best practices when creating content: keyword-rich titles under 60 characters, meta descriptions under 160 characters, proper heading hierarchy, and descriptive image alt text.

articles.title: AEO: AI Search Optimization
articles.question: What is Answer Engine Optimization (AEO)?
articles.answer_text: AEO optimizes content for AI search engines that provide direct answers. FlowWink generates LLMs.txt automatically, and the Knowledge Base is inherently AEO-optimized with question-answer format. FlowPilot writes content that is both SEO and AEO friendly — structuring articles around specific questions with direct answers.

articles.title: Setting Objectives for Content
articles.question: How should I configure content objectives for FlowPilot?
articles.answer_text: Be specific with objectives: "Publish 4 blog posts per month about sustainable technology for CTOs" beats "Write some blog posts." Include: topic area, audience, frequency, tone, and any constraints. FlowPilot learns from your editorial feedback — editing a draft teaches it your preferences for future content.

articles.title: Conversion Optimization
articles.question: How do I get more visitors to take action?
articles.answer_text: Clear CTAs: one primary CTA per page, action words, standout buttons. Reduce form friction: ask only essentials. Build trust: testimonials, customer logos, team photos. FlowPilot can analyze page performance and suggest improvements via the AEO analyzer and its reflection cycle.

kbCategories.name: Modules Overview
kbCategories.description: All available modules — each one a channel FlowPilot can operate.

articles.title: Available Modules
articles.question: What modules are available in FlowWink?
articles.answer_text: FlowWink modules (each operable by FlowPilot): Core (always on): Pages, Media Library, Global Elements. Content: Blog, Knowledge Base. Marketing: Newsletter, AI Chat, Forms. Sales: Leads, Deals, Companies, Products, Orders, Booking. System: Analytics, Webhooks, Users, FlowPilot. Toggle modules in Admin → Modules.

articles.title: FlowPilot Module
articles.question: What is the FlowPilot module?
articles.answer_text: The FlowPilot module is the autonomous agent control center. Manage skills, objectives, memory, automations, and activity logs. This is where you configure how your website runs itself — from setting goals to reviewing autonomous actions.

articles.title: Analytics Module
articles.question: What is the Analytics module?
articles.answer_text: Track page views, visitor stats, popular content, traffic sources, and geography. Privacy-focused with no third-party cookies. FlowPilot uses analytics data during its reflection cycle to evaluate content performance and adjust strategies.

articles.title: Webhooks Module
articles.question: What are webhooks?
articles.answer_text: Webhooks notify external services when events occur. Supported events: page/blog published, form submitted, order placed, subscriber added. FlowPilot's signal automations are the internal equivalent — both react to events, but signals trigger skills while webhooks call external URLs.

kbCategories.name: Objectives & Automations
kbCategories.description: Two cadences of autonomous operation — finite goals and recurring routines.

articles.title: Objectives Overview
articles.question: What are FlowPilot objectives and how do they work?
articles.answer_text: Objectives are high-level, finite goals with tracked progress. Create one in Admin → FlowPilot → Objectives. Define the goal, success criteria, and any constraints. FlowPilot decomposes the objective into steps, tracks progress via a structured JSONB field, and marks it complete when all criteria are met. Statuses: active, completed, paused, failed.

articles.title: Automations Overview
articles.question: What are automations and how do I set one up?
articles.answer_text: Automations are recurring skill executions on a cron schedule. Go to Admin → Engine Room → Automations → New Automation. Select a trigger type (Cron Schedule), set the schedule (e.g., "0 9 * * 1" for every Monday at 9 AM), choose the skill to execute, and configure arguments. Automations run indefinitely until disabled.

articles.title: Objectives vs Automations
articles.question: When should I use an objective versus an automation?
articles.answer_text: Use objectives for finite, strategic work with a clear end state: "Research competitors and write a positioning memo." Use automations for ongoing operational routines: "Monitor competitor blog every Monday." Objectives complete when done. Automations run on schedule forever. They complement each other — an objective might discover that a recurring task needs an automation.

articles.title: Objective Progress Tracking
articles.question: How does FlowPilot track objective progress?
articles.answer_text: Each objective has a success_criteria JSONB field (the definition of done) and a progress JSONB field (current state). FlowPilot updates progress using objective_update_progress and objective_complete tools, creating a structured audit trail. View progress in Admin → FlowPilot → Objectives → click any objective.

articles.title: Cron Schedule Examples
articles.question: What are some common cron schedules for automations?
articles.answer_text: Common schedules: "0 9 * * 1" = every Monday at 9 AM. "0 */12 * * *" = every 12 hours. "0 8 * * 1-5" = weekdays at 8 AM. "0 0 1 * *" = first of every month. "0 9 * * 1,4" = Monday and Thursday at 9 AM. Use these for competitor monitoring, content research, lead scoring, and performance reporting.

kbCategories.name: Agent-to-Agent (A2A)
kbCategories.description: Connect FlowPilot with other AI agents for cross-boundary collaboration.

articles.title: A2A Overview
articles.question: What is Agent-to-Agent (A2A) and how does FlowPilot use it?
articles.answer_text: A2A is a peer-to-peer protocol that lets AI agents discover capabilities, exchange tasks, and collaborate across organizations. FlowPilot supports A2A natively — register peers in Admin → System → A2A Peers. Each peer has a URL endpoint, capabilities manifest, and separate inbound/outbound authentication tokens.

articles.title: Registering an A2A Peer
articles.question: How do I connect FlowPilot to another AI agent?
articles.answer_text: Go to Admin → System → A2A Peers → Add Peer. Enter the peer name, URL endpoint, and capabilities. An outbound token is generated automatically. Share your inbound endpoint and token with the peer for bidirectional communication. Test the connection before enabling production traffic.

articles.title: A2A Security
articles.question: How is A2A communication secured?
articles.answer_text: Each A2A peer uses separate inbound and outbound tokens. Inbound tokens are stored as hashes (never plaintext). Outbound tokens are generated using cryptographic random bytes. All communication is logged in a2a_activity with direction, payload, status, and duration for full auditability.

articles.title: A2A Activity Log
articles.question: How do I monitor A2A interactions?
articles.answer_text: Every A2A interaction is logged in the a2a_activity table. View activity in Admin → System → A2A Activity. Each entry shows: peer name, direction (inbound/outbound), skill name, input/output data, status (pending/success/failed), duration in milliseconds, and any error messages.

kbCategories.name: Content API
kbCategories.description: Consume your content via REST/GraphQL endpoints — headless CMS capabilities.

articles.title: Content API Overview
articles.question: What is the Content API and how do I use it?
articles.answer_text: The Content API (Admin → System → Content API) provides 20+ REST/GraphQL endpoints for consuming your content programmatically. Use it for mobile apps, external websites, newsletters, and AI integrations. The API Explorer lets you test endpoints interactively. Content is served in standardized Tiptap JSON format.

articles.title: API Explorer
articles.question: How do I test Content API endpoints?
articles.answer_text: Navigate to Admin → System → Content API. The interactive API Explorer shows all available endpoints with request/response previews. Click any endpoint to see the URL, parameters, and live response data. Copy endpoint URLs directly for use in your applications.

articles.title: Markdown Export for LLMs
articles.question: Can I export content as Markdown for AI/LLM consumption?
articles.answer_text: Yes. The Content API supports Markdown export optimized for LLMs. This includes LLMs.txt generation for AI search engine optimization (AEO). FlowWink automatically structures content for both traditional search engines and AI answer engines.

kbCategories.name: FlowAgent Framework
kbCategories.description: The 7-step autonomous reasoning engine that powers FlowPilot.

articles.title: The 7-Step Loop
articles.question: What is the FlowAgent 7-step loop?
articles.answer_text: FlowAgent implements a 7-step autonomous reasoning loop: 1) Self-Heal — check system health, retry failed operations. 2) Propose — evaluate objectives and suggest next actions. 3) Plan — decompose proposals into specific skill calls. 4) Advance — execute skills via agent-execute. 5) Automate — identify patterns that should become recurring automations. 6) Reflect — evaluate what worked and what failed. 7) Remember — store learnings in persistent memory.

articles.title: Shared Reasoning Engine
articles.question: What is the agent-reason shared module?
articles.answer_text: The agent-reason module is the centralized logic core that eliminates duplication between interactive tasks (Operate mode — responding to chat/commands) and autonomous tasks (Heartbeat — scheduled cycles). It handles unified LLM orchestration, context loading (Soul, Identity, Memories, Objectives, Instructions), and the iterative reasoning loop. This ensures consistent decision-making regardless of trigger.

articles.title: Built-in Tools
articles.question: What tools does the FlowAgent reasoning engine have access to?
articles.answer_text: FlowAgent has 26+ built-in tools across categories: Ecommerce (product management, order tracking), CRM (lead qualification, company enrichment, deal management), Content (blog writing, page creation, KB updates), Analytics (performance review, conversion analysis), System (soul update, skill instruct, memory management), and A2A (peer communication). The tool registry is database-driven and extensible.

articles.title: Signal Ingest API
articles.question: How do external events trigger FlowPilot?
articles.answer_text: The Signal Ingest API accepts token-authenticated events from external sources via the signal-dispatcher edge function. Database triggers fire signals for internal events (lead created, form submitted, booking made, blog published). Signals trigger matching automations, which execute skills. This creates a reactive layer on top of the proactive objective system.

kbCategories.name: Webinars
kbCategories.description: Host and manage webinar events with registration, scheduling, and follow-up.

articles.title: Webinars Overview
articles.question: How do webinars work in FlowWink?
articles.answer_text: The Webinars module lets you create events with registration forms, scheduling, and attendee management. Add webinar blocks to any page to display upcoming events. FlowPilot can manage event promotion, registration confirmations, and post-event follow-ups through its skill engine.

articles.title: Creating a Webinar
articles.question: How do I create and promote a webinar?
articles.answer_text: Go to Admin → Webinars → New Webinar. Set the title, date, time, description, and registration link. Add a Webinar block to any page to display it. FlowPilot can automatically promote webinars via blog posts, newsletter campaigns, and chat recommendations to site visitors.

kbCategories.name: Consultant Profiles
kbCategories.description: Manage consultant and team member profiles for agency and consulting templates.

articles.title: Consultant Profiles Overview
articles.question: What are consultant profiles and how do they work?
articles.answer_text: Consultant Profiles let you showcase team members with skills, experience, certifications, hourly rates, and availability status. Used in consulting and agency templates. Profiles support education history, portfolio links, LinkedIn URLs, and multi-language capabilities. Visitors can browse and filter consultants by skill, availability, and expertise.

articles.title: Resume Matcher
articles.question: What is the Resume Matcher feature?
articles.answer_text: The Resume Matcher block allows visitors to upload a job description or requirements document, and FlowPilot matches it against your consultant profiles — scoring relevance based on skills, experience, and availability. This automates the talent-matching process for staffing agencies and consulting firms.

data.title: Traditional ERP Reacts. FlowWink Acts.
data.content:
Every ERP you've used is a form waiting to be filled. A workflow waiting to be triggered. A dashboard waiting to be read. They digitize processes — but you still run them.

FlowWink inverts this entirely. It's an ERP where agentic intelligence is native — not a plugin, not a module, not a chatbot in the corner. The process engine itself has objectives, memory, and skills.

It doesn't wait for a purchase order to be created. It identifies the need, drafts the order, and routes it for approval. That's the difference between a system that reacts and one that acts.
data.secondaryContent:
**Traditional ERP**
Event → Human notices → Human acts → Result

**FlowWink BOS**
Objective set → Agent monitors → Agent acts → Human approves

It operates your entire digital presence — content, CRM, email, bookings — continuously and autonomously.

items.question: Is it safe to let an AI run my website?

content.text: Yes. FlowPilot has a full human-in-the-loop system. Every skill can be configured to require approval before execution. Every action is logged in the activity feed with full audit trails. You control what's autonomous and what requires your sign-off.
Yes! FlowWink is open source under the MIT license. You get the full FlowPilot agent, all skills, persistent memory — everything. The only costs are your own hosting and AI model API fees.

items.question: Can I use my own AI model?

content.text: Absolutely. FlowWink supports OpenAI, Google Gemini, and local LLMs via Ollama or any OpenAI-compatible endpoint. Your data stays on your infrastructure for complete privacy.

items.question: Can I migrate from self-hosted to managed?

content.text: Yes. We provide migration tools to move your content, agent memory, and settings to our managed infrastructure. The process is seamless.

items.question: How is this different from ChatGPT + WordPress?

content.text: ChatGPT is a conversation tool. FlowPilot is an operator. It has persistent memory that survives across sessions, objectives it tracks toward completion, skills it can execute autonomously, and a self-evolution mechanism. It's not just answering questions — it's running your business.

content.text: Every FlowPilot instance has a soul — a persistent identity document that defines who it is, what it values, and how it behaves. The soul isn't a static prompt. It evolves based on experience.
When FlowPilot writes a blog post, it doesn't just follow instructions. It writes as itself — with a voice shaped by hundreds of past interactions, feedback loops, and self-reflections.
This is the difference between a tool and an operator.
🧠 Soul Components
Identity
— Name, role, core personality traits
Values
— What it prioritizes (accuracy, speed, empathy)
Communication Style
— How it writes, responds, and interacts
Boundaries
— What it won't do, even if asked
Self-Image
— How it describes itself to visitors

content.text: Yes! Unlike cloud-based AI services, our Private AI runs entirely on our own HIPAA-compliant servers. Your conversations and health questions never leave our secure infrastructure.

items.question: How do I book an appointment?

content.text: You can book appointments online 24/7 using our booking system. Simply select your service, choose an available time, and confirm. You'll receive an email confirmation immediately.
We accept most major insurance plans including Medicare, Blue Cross Blue Shield, Aetna, Cigna, and United Healthcare. Contact us to verify your specific coverage before your visit.

items.question: How do I access my medical records?

content.text: You can access your medical records through our secure patient portal. We use two-factor authentication and encrypted connections to protect your privacy.
Comprehensive primary care including annual physicals, chronic disease management, preventive screenings, and wellness consultations. Your first point of contact for all health concerns.
Board-certified specialists in cardiology, dermatology, orthopedics, and neurology. Referrals coordinated seamlessly through our integrated system — no paperwork, no delays.
Secure video consultations from the comfort of your home. HIPAA-compliant, end-to-end encrypted, and available same-day for urgent non-emergency concerns.
